title: Glaze firing schedule: cone 6
subtitle: A standard mid-fire glaze firing cycle for stoneware at approximately 1222°C
excerpt: A cone 6 glaze firing melts the glaze and vitrifies a stoneware body at approximately 1222°C. The schedule climbs through three phases: a controlled rise through quartz inversion, a steady mid-temperature climb, and a slow final approach to cone bend with a hold at peak. Cooling is unforced and the kiln stays closed until the temperature drops below 80°C. This tutorial covers the schedule for a single-layer cone 6 glaze on smooth stoneware.
sourceNotes: Cone 6 glaze firing schedule derived from first principles and standard studio practice. Orton Ceramic Foundation pyrometric cone reference for cone 6 = approximately 1222°C at a 60°C-per-hour final climb. Encyclopaedia Britannica, eleventh edition (1911), ceramics entry on the historical development of mid-fire stoneware schedules.

glossaryTerms.definition: The second kiln firing at the body's mature temperature that melts the glaze and vitrifies the body. For mid-fire stoneware this is cone 6 (1222°C).
A pyrometric measurement of heat-work (the combination of temperature and time). Cone 6 bends at approximately 1222°C at a final climb of 60°C per hour.
The fusing of the clay body into a glass-like state at full kiln temperature. Stoneware vitrifies at cone 6; the finished body holds water without a glaze.
A physical change in silica at approximately 573°C where the crystalline structure rearranges. Rapid heating or cooling through this point causes cracking.
Pottery fired once at cone 06 (999°C); hard and porous, ready for glazing.

content.text: A
glaze firing

at cone 6 melts the glaze on a stoneware piece and brings the body to vitrification. The kiln climbs from room temperature through approximately 1222°C, with controlled rates through key temperature zones, a brief hold at peak, and an unforced cool-down. This is the standard mid-fire schedule for a single-layer cone 6 glaze on smooth stoneware bisqueware. The schedule applies equally to grogged and speckled stoneware bodies fired at cone 6. Access to an electric kiln rated for cone 6 is required.

attrs.title: Kiln safety
attrs.body: Silica dust is the chronic studio hazard and silicosis is irreversible. Wet-clean only; no sweeping. For the firing itself: ventilation matters. Either a downdraft vent on the kiln or an open window with an extractor pulling air past the kiln. Wear welder's shade-5 glasses when peephole-watching at high temperatures; the cobalt and lithium flash at cone bend is bright enough to leave after-images. Never open a hot kiln below 80°C; thermal shock cracks the work and any reduction firing leaves carbon monoxide in the chamber. Keep a Class A fire extinguisher within reach of the studio area. Keep flammable material (wood, paper, rags) at least 1 m from the kiln casing.

content.text: What cone 6 means
A
cone

measures heat-work (temperature combined with time at temperature) rather than temperature alone. Cone 6 bends at approximately 1222°C when the final climb is at 60°C per hour. A faster climb pushes the bend temperature slightly higher; a slower climb brings it lower. Most modern electric kiln controllers accept a target temperature and ramp rate rather than a cone number directly, so the studio sets the controller to 1222°C with the standard ramps.

Place a witness cone (cone 6) on the shelf at mid-height in the kiln, visible through the peephole. The witness cone gives a physical confirmation that the target heat-work has been reached. A bent witness cone tells the studio cone 6 was reached; a still-upright cone signals the firing was short of target.
Loading the kiln

Glaze-fired pieces cannot touch each other or the kiln furniture during firing. The glaze melts at peak and any contact will fuse the pieces together permanently. Leave 15-20 mm of clearance between glazed pieces and to the kiln walls. Place pieces with the heavier glaze coats lower in the kiln; runs are easier to manage on a lower shelf.

Coat every kiln shelf with a fresh layer of kiln wash before each glaze firing. The wash protects the shelf from glaze drips and lets a run be chipped off rather than fired in permanently. Place pyrometric cones (typically a cone 5, a cone 6, and a cone 7) at mid-height on a shelf, visible through the peephole. The cone 5 bends first as a warning the firing is approaching target; the cone 6 confirms target; the cone 7 (if it bends) signals an overshoot.
The schedule
1. Initial climb: room temperature to 200°C

Climb at 80°C per hour to 200°C. The glaze layer is dry but still holds a small amount of residual water; this slow climb drives off the water without bubbling the glaze coat. Skip the slow phase only if the glaze has been on the
bisqueware
overnight or longer.
2. Mid climb: 200°C to 570°C

Climb at 150°C per hour to 570°C. The kiln passes through the temperature zone where wax resist (if used) burns off; visible smoke and a faint smell are normal between 200-400°C. Glaze materials begin to sinter but do not yet flow.
3. Through quartz inversion: 570°C to 620°C
Slow to 80°C per hour through the
quartz inversion
zone. The silica in the clay body rearranges its crystalline structure at 573°C, and the body expands by about 1%. Rapid heating through this zone causes cracking; slow heating keeps the body intact.
4. High climb: 620°C to 1100°C
Climb at 150°C per hour to 1100°C. The glaze begins to melt in this zone; the powdered surface goes glassy and the colour starts to develop. The body is sintering progressively, on its way to full
vitrification
5. Final approach: 1100°C to 1222°C

Slow to 60°C per hour for the final approach to cone 6. The slower final climb gives the glaze more time at high temperature, which means a more even melt and a smoother final surface. A faster final climb undermelts; a slower final climb risks an overshoot if the controller does not cut off precisely at target.
6. Hold at 1222°C: 10-15 minutes

Hold at 1222°C for 10-15 minutes. The hold evens out the temperature across the kiln (corners and edges tend to be slightly cooler than the centre) and gives the cone time to bend. Watch the witness cone through the peephole during the hold; when the cone tip touches the kiln shelf, the firing is complete. Switch off.
7. Cool-down

Allow the kiln to cool with the lid closed, no forced ventilation. The natural cool-down for an electric kiln from 1222°C to room temperature takes 12-18 hours depending on kiln size and insulation. The slower cool-down through quartz inversion (going down through 573°C this time) protects against thermal shock cracking in the body. Some studios slow the cool-down at this zone with a controlled hold; the schedule above gives a clean result without any cooling holds.
Open the kiln only when the temperature reads 80°C or below. Opening hotter risks thermal shock to the work; opening with the lid lifted on a still-hot kiln invites a face full of hot air and steam.
Schedule summary
Total firing time from start to switch-off: approximately 9-10 hours. Total cool-down to 80°C: approximately 12-18 hours. From kiln close to kiln open: 21-28 hours, or one day plus overnight.
After the firing

Unload onto a clean board. Inspect each piece for runs onto the kiln shelf; chip any glaze drops off the kiln wash with a chisel and re-coat the affected shelf area. Inspect the witness cones; cone 6 bent flat confirms the target was reached. A still-upright cone 6 with a fully bent cone 5 means the firing was just short of target; on the next firing add 10-15 minutes to the peak hold to push the heat-work further.
What it looks like when it is right

Pieces from a correctly run cone 6 glaze firing have smooth glossy or matte surfaces (depending on the glaze), no pinholing, no crawling, and no runs onto the kiln shelf. The body rings cleanly when tapped. The witness cone 6 is bent flat with the tip touching the shelf; cone 5 is fully bent; cone 7 is upright (no overshoot). The kiln shelves are clean apart from light wear on the kiln wash.
Common mistakes
Loading too tight. Glazed pieces touch in the firing and fuse together. The minimum clearance between pieces is 15-20 mm; check by hand before closing the kiln.
Climbing too fast through the early phase. The glaze layer holds residual water; a fast climb bubbles the glaze and pinholes the finished surface. Take the 80°C-per-hour climb to 200°C seriously.

Skipping the witness cones in favour of the pyrometer alone. The pyrometer reads temperature but not heat-work; the cone bends only when both temperature and time accumulate to the target. A pyrometer-only firing can read 1222°C without actually completing cone 6.
Forcing the cool-down by cracking the lid. The thermal shock cracks pieces inside and chips glaze surfaces. Cool unforced with the lid closed.

items.symptom: Witness cone 6 still upright after the firing
items.cause: Heat-work fell short of cone 6; climb too fast, hold too short, or thermocouple drift
items.fix: Add 10-15 minutes to the peak hold on the next firing; check the thermocouple calibration against a known cone if persistent

items.symptom: Pieces stuck to the kiln shelf
items.cause: Glaze ran onto the shelf during firing; kiln wash inadequate or thin
items.fix: Coat the shelf with two coats of kiln wash, brushed at right angles; chip the stuck piece off carefully with a chisel and grind the foot smooth

items.cause: Climb too fast through the early phase; trapped gas in the glaze coat
items.fix: Slow the initial climb to 80°C per hour up to 200°C; ensure the glaze coat was fully dry before loading

items.symptom: Glaze crawled in patches, leaving bare bisque
items.cause: Dust or grease on the bisque before glazing, or glaze applied too thick
items.fix: Wipe bisque with a damp sponge before glazing; check glaze specific gravity is 1.45 or below for dipping; reduce dip time to one second

items.cause: Rapid cooling through quartz inversion at 573°C, or pieces loaded too close to a kiln wall
items.fix: Cool unforced with the lid fully closed; keep at least 25 mm clearance between work and kiln walls
